cuisine: Sports Nutrition Supplement / Clinical Medical Nutrition
region_of_origin: Global (synthesised; Creapure gold-standard manufactured in Trostberg, Germany)
food_subgroup: Sports and clinical nutrition supplement — creatine monohydrate; phosphocreatine system energy buffer for high-intensity exercise

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for creatine monohydrate supplement. Closest parent concept: nitrogen-containing organic acid derivative used as a food-grade supplement. FoodOn does not currently index synthetic sports-nutrition compounds at this specificity.
ontology.foodex2_description: Creatine monohydrate, food/pharmaceutical grade, supplement (nitrogen-containing organic acid derivative) — no exact FoodEx2 code available; closest parent category is food supplements / amino acid derivatives
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for creatine monohydrate supplement. Classified under food supplements / nitrogen-containing organic acid derivatives as closest parent. Code intentionally left null to avoid fabrication.
ontology.snomed_ct_description: Creatine monohydrate supplement — no exact SNOMED CT food code; substance concept exists under dietary supplement category but specific food-record code not confirmed
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT substance code for creatine exists (e.g., creatine as a substance) but a food-record-level SNOMED CT code is not confirmed in approved sources; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of creatine monohydrate is 5g (approximately one level teaspoon or one scoop), typically dissolved in water or a beverage and consumed once daily.

nutrient_source.title: Creatine Monohydrate — USDA FoodData Central; EFSA Scientific Opinion on the safety of creatine monohydrate 2004/2022; ISSN Position Stand on Creatine Supplementation 2017
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central / European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) / International Society of Sports Nutrition (ISSN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Creatine monohydrate is a single-ingredient supplement with a well-characterised chemical composition. All macronutrient values (0 kcal, 0g protein, 0g fat, 0g carbohydrate per 100g) are confirmed by USDA FoodData Central and EFSA safety opinion. The compound is a nitrogen-containing organic acid that is not metabolised for energy and contains no conventional macronutrients. ISSN 2017 Position Stand confirms dosing and safety profile.

gi_source.title: N/A — Creatine monohydrate contains no digestible carbohydrates; glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.organization: EFSA Scientific Opinion on creatine monohydrate safety 2004/2022; USDA FoodData Central
gi_source.source_match_notes: Creatine monohydrate is a nitrogen-containing organic acid with zero digestible carbohydrate content. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. No GI testing is possible or meaningful for this compound. GL per 5g serving = 0.0 by definition.

hypertension.key_factor: Zero sodium content; no direct hemodynamic effect
hypertension.guidance: Creatine monohydrate contains zero sodium and has no direct antihypertensive or hypertensive effect; when combined with resistance exercise it may indirectly improve cardiovascular risk factors through improved lean mass and metabolic function, making it safe for use in hypertensive patients at standard 5g/day dosing.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI not applicable (zero carbohydrate); may improve glycaemic control via GLUT4 upregulation and lean mass accretion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Creatine supplementation combined with resistance exercise has been shown in RCT evidence (Gualano et al. 2011) to reduce HbA1c by approximately 0.3% more than resistance exercise alone in T2DM patients; however, creatine elevates serum creatinine by 10–20 μmol/L causing falsely low eGFR readings — clinicians must use cystatin C-based eGFR to monitor renal function in T2DM patients taking creatine, and creatine is contraindicated in established CKD (eGFR less than 60 mL/min/1.73m²).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero atherogenic nutrients; lean mass and exercise capacity benefits may reduce cardiovascular risk indirectly
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Creatine monohydrate contains no sodium, saturated fat, cholesterol, or trans fat, and at standard 5g/day dosing poses no direct cardiovascular risk; resistance exercise enabled by creatine supplementation improves lean mass, reduces visceral adiposity, and may indirectly reduce cardiovascular risk factors, though creatine should be used with caution in patients with severe heart failure due to limited safety data in that population.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sarcopenia risk in RA; creatine plus resistance exercise is the most evidence-based sarcopenia intervention
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients face elevated sarcopenia risk from chronic inflammation and corticosteroid use; creatine combined with resistance exercise is the most evidence-based intervention for sarcopenia prevention and reversal, but renal function must be monitored given frequent NSAID use, rare amyloidosis, and methotrexate nephrotoxicity in this population.

chronic_kidney_disease.key_factor: Creatine elevates serum creatinine and is contraindicated in established CKD
chronic_kidney_disease.guidance: Creatine monohydrate is contraindicated in patients with established CKD (eGFR less than 60 mL/min/1.73m²) as it elevates serum creatinine by 10–20 μmol/L causing falsely low eGFR readings that may mask disease progression; if creatine use is considered in borderline renal function, cystatin C-based eGFR must be used for monitoring.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Creatine monohydrate (food/pharmaceutical grade) is a single-ingredient sports and clinical nutrition supplement providing 0 kcal per 5g serving. It is a nitrogen-containing organic acid (N-amidino-N-methyl-glycine monohydrate) that is not metabolised for energy and contains no protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, or micronutrients in conventional nutritional terms. The compound replenishes phosphocreatine (PCr) stores in skeletal muscle, enabling faster regeneration of ATP during explosive, high-intensity exercise (sprinting, weightlifting, HIIT). Standard dosing is 5g once daily (no loading phase required per current ISSN 2017 guidance). EFSA 2022 confirms 3g/day is safe long-term in healthy adults; ISSN 2017 Position Stand identifies creatine monohydrate as the most evidence-based sports supplement with over 500 supporting studies. Key clinical benefits: (1) Strength and power output improvement in resistance training; (2) Lean mass gains of approximately 0.98kg more than exercise alone (Cochrane 2022 meta-analysis); (3) Sarcopenia prevention in elderly populations; (4) Potential glycaemic benefit in T2DM via GLUT4 upregulation. CRITICAL CLINICAL WARNINGS: (1) WATER RETENTION — expect 0.5–1kg body weight gain in the first week from intramuscular water retention; inform patients to prevent concern; (2) RENAL CAVEAT — creatine elevates serum creatinine by 10–20 μmol/L causing falsely low eGFR; use cystatin C-based GFR in all creatine users; CONTRAINDICATED in established CKD (eGFR less than 60). Vegan and vegetarian users show 2–3 times greater response than omnivores due to lower baseline muscle creatine stores. Pharmaceutical-grade Creapure (AlzChem Trostberg, Germany) is the gold-standard form. Available in Singapore at GNC, Guardian, NTUC (OXO brand), Lazada, and hospital pharmacies. Dietary flags: vegan, vegetarian, halal, gluten-free, dairy-free.
common_user_queries: best creatine supplement Singapore GNC Guardian

confidence_reason: Creatine monohydrate is a single-ingredient supplement with a fully characterised chemical composition confirmed by USDA FoodData Central, EFSA Scientific Opinion 2004/2022, and ISSN Position Stand 2017. All macronutrient values (zero across all conventional nutrients) are unambiguous and multiply confirmed. Clinical annotations are drawn from peer-reviewed RCT and meta-analysis evidence. Confidence is high.
notes: ZERO MACRO JUSTIFICATION (g4): protein_g is confirmed zero — creatine monohydrate is a nitrogen-containing organic acid that is not a dietary protein source and contains no amino acid chains; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central and EFSA 2004/2022. fat_g is confirmed zero — pure creatine monohydrate contains no lipid fraction; confirmed zero by formulation and USDA FoodData Central. carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero — creatine monohydrate contains no digestible carbohydrate; it is not a sugar, starch, or polysaccharide; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central and EFSA. energy_kcal is confirmed zero — creatine is not metabolised via conventional energy-yielding pathways (not oxidised for ATP via glycolysis, beta-oxidation, or TCA cycle); confirmed zero per EFSA 2004/2022. NEGATIVE SENTINEL (-1): water_g set to -1 as moisture content of creatine monohydrate powder is not reported as a food-composition value in USDA FoodData Central or EFSA food safety opinion; the monohydrate form contains one water molecule per creatine molecule by chemical definition (approximately 12% water by molecular weight) but this is not reported as a nutritional water content value in approved food composition databases. GI NOTE: glycemic_index set to null and gi_category set to not_applicable — creatine monohydrate contains no digestible carbohydrates; glycemic index testing is not applicable or meaningful for this compound. ONTOLOGY NOTE: FoodOn URI, FoodEx2 code, and SNOMED CT food code are all null; no fabricated codes used. Gap notes provided for each. Creatine monohydrate as a food supplement is not indexed at species/product level in FoodOn or FoodEx2 as of 2024. MULTILINGUAL NOTE: Tamil and Hindi local names are not provided as creatine monohydrate is a global synthetic supplement with no established Tamil or Hindi vernacular food name; the supplement is referred to by its English or transliterated name in these language communities in Singapore. CLINICAL PRIORITY WARNINGS: (1) Creatine elevates serum creatinine 10–20 μmol/L — always use cystatin C-based eGFR in creatine users; (2) Contraindicated in CKD eGFR less than 60; (3) Expect 0.5–1kg water retention in week 1 — counsel patients proactively.
